dhanasar.text: PRONG 1 — SUBSTANTIAL MERIT AND NATIONAL IMPORTANCE

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 1 — SUBSTANTIAL MERIT AND NATIONAL IMPORTANCE
dhanasar.argument: 693 requirements mapped clause-by-clause to OMB 2 CFR Part 200, the 2025 OMB Compliance Supplement, and OMB Crosswalk & FAQs.  The Framework covers every OMB provision governing $800B+ in annual federal grant disbursements.  This is not a generic checklist — it is a systematic regulatory architecture that requires deep specialised knowledge to construct.
dhanasar.sources: GAO-23-105678: Internal control deficiencies are the primary cause of federally-funded program audit failures.  PRAC FY2023: $236B in federal improper payments — the Framework addresses documented root causes.
dhanasar.action: ✓ Demonstrated by Sheet ④ Regulatory Coverage — 693 clause-level citations

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 1 — SUBSTANTIAL MERIT AND NATIONAL IMPORTANCE
dhanasar.argument: Federal Audit Clearinghouse FY2022: 30,000+ Single Audit-subject entities annually.  PRAC FY2023: $10B+ in questioned costs concentrated among smaller grantees.  If 5% of these entities adopt this free Framework and reduce audit findings by 10%, the protected federal funds exceed $500M annually.  This calculation uses only PRAC and FAC published data.
dhanasar.sources: Source: PRAC FY2023 Annual Report (p.14); Federal Audit Clearinghouse FY2022 data; GAO-23-105678; HUD OIG FY2023 Annual Report.  Note: Commercial alternatives (eCivis, Blackbaud) charge $15,000–$80,000/year — inaccessible to target population.
dhanasar.action: ⚑ Add National Impact Calculator tab cross-reference (Exhibit 18) before filing

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 1 — SUBSTANTIAL MERIT AND NATIONAL IMPORTANCE
dhanasar.argument: ① Executive Summary: quantified scorecard across 693 requirements.  ④ Regulatory Coverage: 13 domains × 3 regulatory sources = complete national OMB coverage.  ③ Risk Priority Matrix: risk calibrated to PRAC audit failure data — shows the Framework targets the highest-impact compliance gaps.
dhanasar.sources: PRAC FY2023; GAO-23-105678; Federal Audit Clearinghouse FY2022; HUD OIG FY2023
dhanasar.action: ✓ All three sheets ready for USCIS submission

dhanasar.text: PRONG 2 — PETITIONER IS WELL POSITIONED TO ADVANCE THE ENDEAVOUR

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 2 — PETITIONER IS WELL POSITIONED TO ADVANCE THE ENDEAVOUR
dhanasar.argument: FCA (ICAP): Pakistan's highest accounting qualification.  FCCA (ACCA UK): ECCTIS-benchmarked at RQF Level 7 (Master's equivalent).  CFA (CFA Institute USA): U.S.-aligned investment & governance standard.  18+ years: COSO-based internal control design for EMAAR Economic City ($15B+) and Red Sea Global.  The 693-requirement Framework was constructed by personally analysing every OMB 2024 provision — demonstrating applied U.S. regulatory expertise, not just awareness.
dhanasar.sources: See: Carnegie Evaluation (Exhibit); ECCTIS Certificate (Exhibit 17.1); CEO/CFO/COO Letters of Recommendation (Exhibits 17); PWC Tax Partner LOR (Exhibit 17).
dhanasar.action: ✓ Credentials independently verified — multiple exhibit cross-references

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 2 — PETITIONER IS WELL POSITIONED TO ADVANCE THE ENDEAVOUR
dhanasar.element: U.S. Regulatory Expertise — Bridge from International Experience
dhanasar.argument: The Framework maps every provision of OMB 2 CFR Part 200 (OMB Final Rule 2024), the 2025 OMB Compliance Supplement, and OMB Crosswalk & FAQs.  This demonstrates expert knowledge of the specific U.S. federal grant regulatory system governing $800B+ in annual disbursements — not merely general accounting expertise.  International competency bridge: IFRS → U.S. GAAP; COSO (EMAAR) → GAO Green Book; Tadawul governance standards → OMB 2 CFR 200.
dhanasar.sources: ② Compliance Register: 265 structured requirements individually mapped to CFR citations.  ④ Regulatory Coverage: domain-to-CFR cross-reference table.  See also: Exhibit 18 ICM (130 controls mapped to specific CFR clauses).
dhanasar.action: ⚑ Add U.S. Regulatory Transferability sheet to Exhibit 18 before filing

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 2 — PETITIONER IS WELL POSITIONED TO ADVANCE THE ENDEAVOUR
dhanasar.argument: ② Compliance Register: 265 requirements individually structured — systematic U.S. regulatory expertise.  ③ Risk Priority Matrix: risk-calibrated architecture showing professional judgment.  ⑤ Evidence Tracker: operational deployment plan — not just design but active implementation.  ⑦ Version Control: development over 5 months (Jan–Apr 2025) — not a pre-filing artefact.
dhanasar.sources: Expert validation: Obtain letter from U.S. public finance professor (NYU Wagner, Syracuse Maxwell, Indiana SPEA) or GFOA committee member confirming Framework methodology is sound and superior to existing commercial alternatives.
dhanasar.action: ⚑ Expert letter is single highest-impact action for Prong 2 — begin outreach immediately

dhanasar.text: PRONG 3 — BENEFICIAL TO THE U.S. TO WAIVE JOB OFFER / LABOR CERTIFICATION

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 3 — BENEFICIAL TO THE U.S. TO WAIVE JOB OFFER / LABOR CERTIFICATION
dhanasar.element: Why a Job Offer Is Impractical
dhanasar.argument: This Framework is released under Creative Commons CC-BY 4.0 at github.com/bilalgovernance/audit-readiness-framework.  Any U.S. entity may use, adapt, and redistribute it without charge, consulting engagement, or proprietary restriction.  A standard employer-employee relationship cannot encompass this cross-sectoral, public-benefit distribution model — no single employer could direct an employee to freely distribute compliance infrastructure to the general public, including competitors.
dhanasar.sources: USCIS Policy Manual §6.C: 'Labor certification is inappropriate when the petitioner proposes an entrepreneurial, consultative, or systemic endeavour not tied to a specific employment role.'
dhanasar.action: ⚑ Deploy GitHub repository immediately — live URL is essential before filing

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 3 — BENEFICIAL TO THE U.S. TO WAIVE JOB OFFER / LABOR CERTIFICATION
dhanasar.argument: Commercial compliance platforms charge $15,000–$80,000/year (eCivis, Blackbaud, SAP).  This pricing is inaccessible to the Framework's target population: municipalities under 50,000 population, tribal governments, CDFIs, public housing authorities, and nonprofits — the entities PRAC identifies as having the highest audit-finding rates and the least compliance infrastructure.  The petitioner's free Framework fills this gap as a documented public good.  A domestic consultant at a firm would sell proprietary services.  This petitioner distributes governance infrastructure nationally, for free.
dhanasar.sources: Quantified benefit: At 5% adoption across 30,000+ Single Audit entities, the Framework protects ~$500M in federal funds annually (see Exhibit 18 National Impact Calculator).  Source: PRAC FY2023; Federal Audit Clearinghouse; commercial platform pricing (eCivis.com, Blackbaud.com).
dhanasar.action: ✓ Market gap documented — add commercial pricing screenshots before filing

dhanasar.prong: PRONG 3 — BENEFICIAL TO THE U.S. TO WAIVE JOB OFFER / LABOR CERTIFICATION
dhanasar.argument: Open-access licence: CC-BY 4.0 — any entity may use, adapt, redistribute without restriction.  Free distribution: no licensing fee, no consulting requirement, no lock-in — maximum national reach.  Target beneficiaries: under-resourced entities — highest-vulnerability per PRAC and HUD OIG data.  ⑤ Evidence Tracker: GitHub evidence URLs document ongoing free deployment.  Special / Program-Specific domain (Sheet ④): agency-specific coverage (HUD, DOT, HHS, DOE, USDA, FEMA) shows the Framework reaches every segment of the federal grant ecosystem.
dhanasar.sources: RECOMMENDATION: Obtain one Letter of Interest from a U.S.-based organisation (NeighborWorks affiliate, tribal government, CDFI, or public housing authority) confirming the Framework addresses their federal grant compliance needs.  Even one such letter transforms Prong 3 from assertion to independently-confirmed evidence.
dhanasar.action: ⚑ Highest-impact Prong 3 action: LOI from U.S. organisation + GitHub URL + download count

register.statement: State the objectives of Uniform Guidance and attach governance policy.
Define key terms and ensure application forms reflect definitions.
Confirm applicability of Uniform Guidance to program and award types.
Document effective date handling for awards spanning versions.
Map superseded regulations and identify transition requirements.
Publish agency-specific implementation guidance where required.
Document program-specific NOFO requirements and posting procedures.
Confirm OMB approvals and incorporate approval conditions.
Ensure mechanisms to terminate applicants who fail eligibility.
Record effective dates and award applicability per award file.
Outline purpose and scope for administrative requirements.
Classify award types and document funding instrument selection.
Document program planning and design activities for funding requests.
Post public notices for funding opportunities per agency rules.
Include minimum NOFO content and ensure completeness of applications.
Define merit review procedures and scoring criteria for NOFOs.
Perform and document pre-award risk evaluations for applicants.
Ensure standard application requirements are captured consistently.
Record any specific award conditions and special terms.
Document agency review of applicant risk and mitigation actions.
Confirm federal award information included in award instrument.
Record public access rules for federal awards in agency file.
Plan and document FSRS/assistance reporting requirements for subawards.
Implement suspension and debarment checks prior to award.
Document termination authority and process for nonperforming awards.
Confirm compliance with statutory prohibitions (e.g., enemy contracts).
Validate telecom and prohibited vendor checks where applicable.
Ensure energy efficiency standards are documented for capital awards.
Verify unique entity ID (SAM) and maintain registration records.
Document transparency requirements and public reporting obligations.
Clarify transition rules for awards spanning UG versions.
Provide examples of eligibility determination for common program types.
Guidance on documentation required for applicant risk scoring.
Ensure audit testing approach aligns with pre-2024 UG awards.
Record award setup data elements and agency conditions.
Include reporting, disbursement and compliance contact details in award.
Define subaward reporting expectations and templates.
Document suspension/debarment clauses and monitoring steps.
Record termination triggers and closeout obligations in award file.
Include required statutory prohibitions and certifications in award.
Attach applicable environmental and energy conditions for capital awards.
Document telecom exclusion statements where applicable.
Confirm Unique Entity ID and associated award metadata.
Crosswalk changed award terms to internal award templates.
Record award objectives and performance measures in award setup.
Categorize award by funding instrument and terms.
Document NOFO-derived commitments and deliverables.
Ensure award terms reflect program design and statutory requirements.
Part 4
Ensure financial management system meets UG requirements (200.302).
Maintain internal controls consistent with COSO and UG.
Document internal control policies and monitor effectiveness.
Establish payment processes to minimize Federal cash on hand.
Document cost sharing and matching procedures.
Record program income accounting and disposition methods.
Create budget revision approval workflows and logs.
Define period of performance policies and controls.
Document insurance coverage and allocation across programs.
Track real property standards, title and usage restrictions.
Record federal interest in property and lien restrictions.
Establish equipment management policies, tagging and inventory.
Document title and disposition rules for supplies.
Track intangible property and software licensing use restrictions.
Document property trust relationships and stewardship roles.
Ensure procurement by states follows state laws and UG.
Maintain written procurement procedures and oversight.
Document competition requirements and justifications for exceptions.
Define methods of procurement and selection rationale.
Document contracting outreach and S/M/WBE participation.
Map internal control updates required by UG crosswalk to policies.
Clarify internal control documentation required per updated UG FAQs.
Apply domestic preference rules to applicable procurements.
Document cost sharing/matching allocation methodology.
Define payment of subawards and flow-through documentation.
Provide procurement policy on recovered materials.
Document bonding requirements for construction or large procurements.
Include required contract clauses in standard templates.
Document performance reporting requirements and KPIs.
Establish program monitoring and reporting cadence.
Define subrecipient vs contractor distinction and tests.
Set pass-through entity responsibilities and flow-downs.
Create a subrecipient monitoring plan and schedule.
Document record retention and access requirements.
Define procedures for transfer of records to awarding agency.
Adopt electronic records management practices consistent with UG.
Update internal control testing per Compliance Supplement guidance.
Align accounting system capabilities to 200.521 and agency expectations.
Document closeout procedures and required evidence checklist.
Record post-closeout responsibilities and any adjustments.
Maintain procurement procedures that comply with UG standards.
Ensure procurement documentation is retained and auditable.
Implement full and open competition practices where required.
Define micro-purchase and small purchase procedures and thresholds.
Document contracting outreach to diverse suppliers.
Apply domestic preference and Buy America requirements where applicable.
Perform cost or price analyses for procurements above thresholds.
Establish vendor debarment checks and documentation.
Document procurement of recovered materials according to policy.
Include required federal clauses in contracts (Appendix II).
Set contract oversight and acceptance testing processes.
Update procurement SOPs to reflect crosswalked threshold changes.
Document sole-source justification templates per FAQ guidance.
Part 4
Maintain records for micro-purchase distribution and vendor fairness.
Maintain procurement competition logs and exceptions.
Implement sealed bidding, RFP, and competitive proposals procedures.
Document price/cost analysis for construction and services.
Train procurement staff on UG clause flow-down requirements.
Clarify procurement public access and protest handling per FAQs.
Track participation of small and disadvantaged businesses.
Maintain documentation for time-and-materials and cost reimbursable contracts.
Ensure procurement files include evaluation criteria and scoring.
Maintain procurement SOP approvals and version control.
Document procurement protest logs and resolutions.
Part 4
Document crosswalked sole-source thresholds and justification approach.
Maintain cost/price analysis templates and approvals.
Distinguish subrecipient versus contractor and document tests.
Define pass-through entity responsibilities and flow-downs.
Establish subrecipient monitoring policies and schedules.
Require subawards to include federal award identification info.
Confirm requirements for fixed amount subawards and documentation.
Update monitoring thresholds according to crosswalk clarification.
Implement site visit and remote monitoring checklists.
Establish access and record retention policies for subrecipients.
Set remedial and enforcement procedures for noncompliant subs.
Part 4
Implement procedures for fixed-amount awards and performance monitoring.
Track subrecipient audit status and required actions.
Provide examples of subrecipient vs contractor classifications.
Ensure subaward agreements contain required terms from UG.
Establish risk-based monitoring frequencies and documentation.
Track corrective action plans and verification of implementation.
Maintain subrecipient closeout procedures and reconciliations.
Part 3.2
Clarify reporting expectations for pass-through entities per FAQ.
Document obligations for monitoring of programmatic performance.
PROP-009
PROP-010
PROP-014
PROP-016
Confirm costs charged are allowable under UG principles.
Document how cost principles are applied to federal awards.
Ensure composition of total cost follows UG definitions.
Document factors affecting cost allowability.
Apply reasonable cost standards consistently across awards.
Maintain documentation for allocable costs and allocation bases.
Classify direct vs indirect costs and record supporting evidence.
Obtain prior written approvals where UG requires prior approval.
Confirm limitations on allowability and list examples.
Document special considerations for specific cost categories.
Outline collection procedures for unallowable costs.
Maintain methodology for direct and indirect cost identification.
Ensure consistent classification of costs in ledgers.
Document direct cost charging policies and exceptions.
Track indirect cost rate selection and NICRA documentation.
Ensure required certifications for rate elections are filed.
Maintain cost sharing documentation and supporting ledgers.
Record interagency service arrangements and cost recharges.
Document idle facility and idle capacity costs and justification.
Track professional service charges and ensure reasonableness.
Maintain advertising and public relations cost policies.
Document advisory council or committee related costs when allowable.
Maintain policy on alcohol-related costs and exceptions.
Track alumni relations and associated allowable expenses.
Record audit services cost allocation and reporting.
Document bad debt treatment and uncollectible receivables.
Track bonding and related financing costs.
Document communications cost policies and allocations.
Maintain payroll and compensation documentation for personnel costs.
Record fringe benefits allocations and methodology.
Document compensation for personal services and timekeeping.
Track pension and retirement-related cost treatment.
Document depreciation and use allowance accounting.
Maintain donated services valuation and approval evidence.
Track entertainment costs and ensure exclusion where unallowable.
Document equipment and capital expenditures and allowability.
Ensure fines and penalties treatment aligns to UG rules.
Maintain policies on fundraising and investment management costs.
Track goods/services for personal use and ensure exclusion.
Document housing/personal living cost policies and exceptions.
Maintain insurance and indemnification cost allocations.
Clarify program income additions/deductions per crosswalk guidance.
Update cost allocation plan templates to reflect crosswalk changes.
Part 4
Establish performance measurement frameworks and KPIs.
Maintain performance metrics and link to award objectives.
Document cash management and drawdown procedures tied to performance.
Ensure cost sharing is tracked against program outputs.
Record program income application to the award budget.
Define performance reporting formats and frequency.
Document monitoring and reporting of program performance.
Clarify required performance metrics and data elements from FAQs.
Part 4
Implement quality assurance checks on performance data.
Document post-closeout adjustments impacting performance outcomes.
Record recovery and debt collection methods affecting program metrics.
Ensure closeout procedures capture final performance deliverables.
Map performance reporting changes from crosswalk and FAQs to SOPs.
REC-009
REC-010
Maintain readiness for Single Audit requirements when threshold met.
Prepare and submit audit report and reporting package per UG.
Define audit submission timing and auditee responsibilities.
Document auditee responsibilities for providing supporting evidence.
Record basis for audit findings and corrective action procedures.
Integrate crosswalk interpretations into audit readiness procedures.
Part 3
Define audit scope and internal audit coordination tasks.
Prepare auditor selection documentation and independence checks.
Ensure audit reporting package completeness prior to submission.
Maintain auditor responsibilities and engagement letters.
Process audit report submission to federal clearinghouse as required.
Prepare SEFA reconciling schedules and CFDA mapping.
